For general everyday use, you're paying a premium for technology you may not fully benefit from. Brands like Oakley or Ray-Ban offer comparable daily-wear frames at similar or lower prices.
  Q: Can you get prescription lenses in Costa frames?
  A: Yes. Costa offers prescription optical frames (clear lenses) and prescription sunglasses with their 580 technology. Any authorized optical store can fit Costa frames with your prescription. Rx sunglasses use special curved prescription lenses to work with Costa's wrap-style frames.
  Q: What's the difference between Costa 580P and 580G?
  A: 580P uses polycarbonate lenses — lighter, more impact-resistant, less expensive. 580G uses glass — optically clearer, more scratch-resistant, heavier, more expensive. For most people, 580P is the better value. Serious anglers and optical purists prefer 580G.
  Q: Does Costa make clear prescription glasses?
  A: Yes. Costa makes prescription optical frames for everyday clear-lens use, not just sunglasses. Styles lean sporty/outdoor but include some lifestyle options suitable for daily wear.
  Q: How do I know my glasses frame size?
  A: Look inside the temple arm for three numbers (e.g., 52-18-140). The first is lens width in mm, second is bridge width, third is temple length. If you don't have a current pair, an optician at Charm Optical can measure your face to determine the ideal frame dimensions.
  Q: What frame material is most durable?
  A: Titanium is the most durable frame material — it's lightweight, corrosion-resistant, hypoallergenic, and flexes without breaking. For budget durability, TR-90 nylon is nearly indestructible. Acetate offers the best colour variety. Charm Optical carries all three materials across their 500+ frame collection.

## Davis Schneider Glasses: What to Know About This Eyewear Brand
URL: https://glassespedia.ca/articles/davis-schneider-glasses
Category: frames
Definition: Davis Schneider Glasses — An eyewear brand offering refined, European-influenced optical frames and sunglasses characterized by clean lines, quality acetate and metal construction, and understated design. The brand occupies the premium independent segment of the eyewear market.
Summary: Davis Schneider is a niche eyewear brand known for refined European-influenced designs, quality acetate construction, and a minimalist aesthetic. If you're drawn to this style, several mainstream premium brands offer similar design philosophy with wider availability — including Persol, Oliver Peoples, and Moscot.
FAQs:
  Q: Is Davis Schneider a luxury brand?
  A: Davis Schneider sits in the premium independent category — above mainstream brands but below ultra-luxury houses. Their pricing ($250–$500 CAD) reflects quality materials and construction rather than fashion-house licensing premiums. Comparable to Persol, Garrett Leight, and Moscot.
  Q: Where can I buy Davis Schneider glasses in Canada?
  A: Davis Schneider is distributed through select independent optical boutiques. Availability in Canada is limited compared to major brands. Check the brand's website for a retailer locator, or explore similar brands like Persol that are more widely available at Canadian optical stores.
  Q: Can I get prescription lenses in Davis Schneider frames?
  A: Yes. Davis Schneider frames accept standard prescription lenses — single vision, progressive, and all lens types. Any optical retailer with standard edging equipment can fit your prescription into these frames.
  Q: What makes Italian acetate better than regular plastic?
  A: Italian acetate (typically from Mazzucchelli) is a layered, plant-based material with richer colour depth, more comfortable flexibility, and better aging characteristics than injection-moulded plastics. It's hand-polished to a deeper finish and maintains its shape better over time.
  Q: How do I know my glasses frame size?
  A: Look inside the temple arm for three numbers (e.g., 52-18-140). The first is lens width in mm, second is bridge width, third is temple length. If you don't have a current pair, an optician at Charm Optical can measure your face to determine the ideal frame dimensions.
  Q: What frame material is most durable?
  A: Titanium is the most durable frame material — it's lightweight, corrosion-resistant, hypoallergenic, and flexes without breaking. For budget durability, TR-90 nylon is nearly indestructible. Acetate offers the best colour variety. Charm Optical carries all three materials across their 500+ frame collection.

## Different Types of Glasses: A Complete Guide to Every Style and Lens
URL: https://glassespedia.ca/articles/different-types-of-glasses
Category: lenses
Definition: Types of Glasses — The broad classification of eyeglasses based on their lens type (how they correct vision), frame style (aesthetic and structural design), and intended purpose (daily wear, sport, safety, fashion). Most glasses combine elements from all three categories.
Summary: Glasses fall into categories by lens type (single vision, bifocal, progressive, reading), frame style (full-rim, semi-rimless, rimless), and purpose (prescription, sunglasses, safety, sport, computer). Understanding the differences helps you choose the right pair for your prescription and lifestyle.
FAQs:
  Q: What type of glasses should I get for the first time?
  A: Start with a versatile full-rim frame in a classic shape (wayfarer, rectangle, or oval) with single vision lenses and anti-reflective coating. This covers most situations and helps you learn what you like before branching out. Your optometrist can guide you based on your specific prescription.
  Q: Do I need different glasses for computer work?
  A: If you're under 40 with a mild prescription, your regular glasses likely work fine for screens. Over 40, or if you spend 6+ hours at a screen daily, dedicated computer glasses with an intermediate-distance prescription can significantly reduce strain.
  Q: How many pairs of glasses do most people need?
  A: Most people do well with two pairs: everyday prescription glasses and a pair of prescription sunglasses. Add a third pair (computer glasses or sport glasses) if your lifestyle demands it. A backup pair is also wise in case your primary pair breaks.
  Q: What's the difference between bifocals and progressives?
  A: Both correct for distance and near vision. Bifocals have a visible line separating two distinct zones. Progressives transition gradually with no visible line, offering a more natural visual experience and better intermediate-distance vision. Most people today prefer progressives.
  Q: What lens material is best for strong prescriptions?
  A: High-index lenses (1.67 or 1.74) are best for strong prescriptions because they bend light more efficiently, resulting in thinner and lighter lenses. Standard plastic (CR-39) works well for mild prescriptions. Your optician at Charm Optical can recommend the ideal index based on your prescription strength.
  Q: How often should I replace my lenses?
  A: Most optometrists recommend updating your prescription every 1-2 years. Lens coatings (anti-reflective, scratch-resistant) degrade over time even if your prescription hasn't changed. If you notice scratches, haze, or peeling, it's time for new lenses. Book an eye exam at Charm Optical to check your current prescription.

## Do Blue Light Glasses Actually Do Anything? What the Research Says
URL: https://glassespedia.ca/articles/do-blue-light-glasses-actually-do-anything
Category: lenses
Definition: Blue Light Glasses — Eyeglasses with lenses that filter or absorb a portion of blue-violet light (approximately 380–450 nm wavelength) emitted by digital screens, LED lighting, and sunlight. Available as prescription or non-prescription, typically with a slight yellow tint.
Summary: Blue light glasses probably won't prevent eye damage or cure digital eye strain — most strain comes from how you use screens, not the light itself. But some people find the slight warmth of blue-light-filtered lenses more comfortable, and wearing them in the evening may help with sleep if you're sensitive to screen light.
FAQs:
  Q: Are blue light glasses a scam?
  A: They're not a scam, but they're oversold. They do filter some blue light, and some people find them comfortable. The science just doesn't support the big claims about preventing eye damage or curing digital eye strain. They're a minor comfort feature, not a medical necessity.
  Q: Do optometrists recommend blue light glasses?
  A: Most Canadian optometrists say blue light glasses are optional, not essential. They'll offer the coating as an add-on if you want it, but they emphasize proper prescription, screen habits, and the 20-20-20 rule as more effective solutions for screen-related discomfort.
  Q: Can blue light glasses help with sleep?
  A: Potentially yes. Blue light in the 460–480 nm range does suppress melatonin. Wearing blue light glasses or using night mode on devices for 1–2 hours before bed may help you fall asleep faster, especially if you're sensitive to screen light.
  Q: Do blue light glasses have a yellow tint?
  A: Some do, some don't. Higher-filtration lenses have a noticeable warm tint. Modern premium blue light coatings are nearly clear, filtering a smaller percentage of blue light with minimal colour distortion.
  Q: Should I get blue light glasses for my kids?
  A: For children, limiting screen time and ensuring regular outdoor play (which helps prevent myopia progression) is far more effective than blue light glasses. If your child uses screens regularly, a comprehensive eye exam to check for uncorrected vision problems is the better first step.

## Do Blue Light Glasses Work? What the Science Actually Says
URL: https://glassespedia.ca/articles/do-blue-light-glasses-work
Category: lenses
Definition: Blue Light Glasses — Blue light glasses are eyewear with lenses that filter or block a portion of blue light (380–500nm wavelength) emitted by digital screens, LED lighting, and sunlight. They can be prescription or non-prescription.
Summary: Blue light glasses filter 10–50% of high-energy visible (HEV) blue light from screens. Research shows they don't significantly reduce digital eye strain (that's caused by focusing fatigue, not light wavelength), but they may help with sleep if worn 2–3 hours before bed.
FAQs:
  Q: Do blue light glasses actually reduce eye strain?
  A: Current evidence says no — digital eye strain is caused by focusing fatigue and reduced blinking, not blue light specifically. Anti-reflective coatings and the 20-20-20 rule are more effective for screen-related eye strain.
  Q: Are blue light glasses worth it for sleep?
  A: Amber-tinted blue light glasses worn 2–3 hours before bed show promising results for improving sleep. Clear blue light lenses filter too little to make a meaningful difference for sleep.
  Q: Can I add blue light filtering to my prescription glasses?
  A: Yes — most optical stores offer blue light filtering as a lens coating or material add-on. At Charm Optical in Edmonton, you can add it to any prescription order, including single vision and progressive lenses.
  Q: Do optometrists recommend blue light glasses?
  A: Most optometrists say blue light glasses aren't harmful but shouldn't be relied upon for eye strain relief. The Canadian Association of Optometrists recommends screen breaks, proper ergonomics, and regular eye exams as the primary approach to digital eye comfort.
  Q: What lens material is best for strong prescriptions?
  A: High-index lenses (1.67 or 1.74) are best for strong prescriptions because they bend light more efficiently, resulting in thinner and lighter lenses. Standard plastic (CR-39) works well for mild prescriptions. Your optician at Charm Optical can recommend the ideal index based on your prescription strength.
  Q: How often should I replace my lenses?
  A: Most optometrists recommend updating your prescription every 1-2 years. Lens coatings (anti-reflective, scratch-resistant) degrade over time even if your prescription hasn't changed. If you notice scratches, haze, or peeling, it's time for new lenses. Book an eye exam at Charm Optical to check your current prescription.

## Do I Need Glasses? Signs, Self-Checks, and When to See an Optometrist
URL: https://glassespedia.ca/articles/do-i-need-glasses
Category: eye-conditions
Definition: Refractive Error — A condition where the eye cannot focus light correctly on the retina, resulting in blurred vision. The four types are myopia (nearsightedness), hyperopia (farsightedness), astigmatism (irregular corneal curvature), and presbyopia (age-related loss of near focus). All are correctable with glasses, contacts, or surgery.
Summary: Common signs you might need glasses: squinting to see clearly, headaches after reading or screen work, difficulty with night driving, holding your phone farther away, and eye fatigue. Online vision tests can hint at a problem but can't replace a comprehensive eye exam. In Canada, most insurance plans cover exams, and kids under 18 get coverage through provincial health plans.
FAQs:
  Q: Can you suddenly need glasses?
  A: Yes. Vision changes can happen gradually (you don't notice until it's significant) or relatively quickly (hormonal changes, diabetes, certain medications). Presbyopia — the need for reading glasses — develops noticeably in your early to mid-40s and feels quite sudden even though it's been progressing for years.
  Q: Do I need glasses if I only have trouble seeing at night?
  A: Possibly. Night vision problems often indicate mild myopia or astigmatism that your brain compensates for in good lighting but can't overcome in the dark. An eye exam will determine if glasses would help, especially for driving.
  Q: How often should I get an eye exam?
  A: The Canadian Association of Optometrists recommends annually for children and seniors, and every 1–2 years for adults with no known issues. More frequently if you have diabetes, a family history of eye disease, or notice vision changes.
  Q: Can wearing glasses make your eyes worse?
  A: No. This is a common myth. Glasses correct your vision but don't change the underlying eye structure. What happens is that once you experience clear vision with glasses, your brain notices the blur without them more than it did before — but your eyes haven't gotten worse.
  Q: Are online eye tests accurate?
  A: Online screening tools can flag potential issues but cannot replace a comprehensive eye exam. They can't measure your prescription precisely, check eye pressure, examine the retina, or assess binocular vision. Use them as motivation to book a real exam, not as a substitute.

## Do Blue Light Glasses Work? What the Research Actually Says
URL: https://glassespedia.ca/articles/does-blue-light-glasses-work
Category: lenses
Definition: Blue Light Glasses — Blue light glasses (also called blue light blocking glasses or blue cut lenses) are eyeglasses with lenses that filter or absorb a portion of blue-violet light (approximately 415-455nm wavelength) emitted by digital screens, LED lighting, and sunlight. They are marketed primarily for reducing digital eye strain and improving sleep quality.
Summary: Blue light glasses filter a portion of blue-violet light from screens and artificial lighting. Research shows they don't significantly reduce digital eye strain (which is caused by focusing, not light wavelength), but they may offer a small benefit for sleep if worn in the evening.
FAQs:
  Q: Do optometrists recommend blue light glasses?
  A: Most optometrists take a measured position. The Canadian Association of Optometrists acknowledges that there's no strong evidence blue light glasses reduce eye strain or prevent eye damage from screens. However, many optometrists will offer blue light coating as an option if a patient wants it, noting that it's harmless and some people report subjective comfort improvements.
  Q: Are blue light glasses the same as computer glasses?
  A: No. Computer glasses are prescription lenses optimized for your specific screen distance and may include progressive or single vision designs. Blue light glasses simply filter certain wavelengths and don't address focusing distance. Computer glasses are far more effective for reducing digital eye strain. Ask about computer lenses at Charm Optical.
  Q: Can kids wear blue light glasses?
  A: Blue light glasses won't harm children's eyes, but they're not a substitute for healthy screen habits. The Canadian Paediatric Society recommends limiting screen time and encouraging outdoor play — which is far more evidence-based than any lens coating for protecting children's eye health.
  Q: How much do blue light glasses cost in Canada?
  A: If added as a coating to prescription lenses, blue light filtering typically costs $30-80 extra. Non-prescription blue light glasses are available for $15-50 at most retailers. The more effective investment is usually a proper pair of computer glasses with the right prescription.
  Q: What lens material is best for strong prescriptions?
  A: High-index lenses (1.67 or 1.74) are best for strong prescriptions because they bend light more efficiently, resulting in thinner and lighter lenses. Standard plastic (CR-39) works well for mild prescriptions. Your optician at Charm Optical can recommend the ideal index based on your prescription strength.
  Q: How often should I replace my lenses?
  A: Most optometrists recommend updating your prescription every 1-2 years. Lens coatings (anti-reflective, scratch-resistant) degrade over time even if your prescription hasn't changed. If you notice scratches, haze, or peeling, it's time for new lenses. Book an eye exam at Charm Optical to check your current prescription.

## G Is for Glasses: An A-to-Z Glossary of Eyewear Terms
URL: https://glassespedia.ca/articles/g-is-for-glasses
Category: lenses
Definition: Glasses Glossary — A comprehensive reference of eyewear terminology — from lens types and frame materials to prescription abbreviations and coating technologies — explained in everyday language.
Summary: A comprehensive but readable glossary covering 50+ eyewear terms from A to Z. Written in plain English so you can walk into any optical store and understand what they're talking about.
FAQs:
  Q: What does OD and OS mean on my prescription?
  A: OD stands for Oculus Dexter (right eye) and OS stands for Oculus Sinister (left eye). They're Latin abbreviations used on every eyeglass prescription. Your right eye is always listed first.
  Q: What's the difference between polycarbonate and high-index lenses?
  A: Polycarbonate is impact-resistant (great for kids and sports) while high-index is thinner and lighter (great for strong prescriptions). For safety, polycarbonate wins. For aesthetics and comfort with a strong prescription, high-index wins. Your optician at Charm Optical can recommend the best fit.
  Q: What does the sphere number on my prescription mean?
  A: Sphere (SPH) is the main lens power. A minus number (like -2.50) corrects nearsightedness. A plus number (like +1.75) corrects farsightedness. Higher numbers mean a stronger prescription. Zero or blank means no correction needed for that measurement.
  Q: Where can I get glasses in Edmonton?
  A: Charm Optical at 5035 Ellerslie Rd SW in South Edmonton carries 500+ designer frames. They offer comprehensive eye exams, same-day glasses, professional fitting, and direct insurance billing for most Alberta providers. Book online at see.charmoptical.ca.
  Q: Does insurance cover glasses in Alberta?
  A: Most employer health plans cover $200-$500 toward glasses every 1-2 years. Alberta Blue Cross, Canada Life, and Desjardins are common providers. Charm Optical direct-bills most Alberta insurers so you only pay the difference.

## Glasses Cleaner: Best Products and DIY Solutions for Streak-Free Lenses
URL: https://glassespedia.ca/articles/glasses-cleaner
Category: care
Definition: Glasses Cleaner — Glasses cleaner refers to any solution or product designed to safely remove oils, fingerprints, dust, and smudges from eyeglass lenses without damaging lens coatings (anti-reflective, blue light, scratch-resistant) or the frame material.
Summary: The safest glasses cleaner is a drop of dish soap (Dawn or similar, no moisturizers) and lukewarm water, dried with a clean microfiber cloth. Commercial lens sprays work well too. Avoid Windex, rubbing alcohol, paper towels, and shirt fabric — all can damage AR coatings.
FAQs:
  Q: Can I use Windex to clean my glasses?
  A: No — Windex contains ammonia which degrades anti-reflective and other lens coatings over time. Use a dedicated lens cleaning spray or a drop of plain dish soap with lukewarm water instead.
  Q: What is the best homemade glasses cleaner?
  A: One drop of plain dish soap (Dawn Original or similar, no moisturizers) with lukewarm water is the safest and most effective DIY glasses cleaner. Rinse, soap, rub gently, rinse again, and dry with a clean microfiber cloth.
  Q: How do I clean glasses without scratching them?
  A: Always rinse under running water first to remove dust particles. Never wipe dry lenses. Use only microfiber cloths — never paper towels, tissues, or clothing. The rinse step is the most important scratch-prevention measure.
  Q: Can I get my glasses cleaned at an optical store?
  A: Yes — most optical stores offer free ultrasonic cleaning, including Charm Optical in South Edmonton. It takes about 2 minutes and cleans areas you can't reach with a cloth. No appointment needed.
  Q: Can an optician fix scratched lenses?
  A: Scratched lenses cannot be repaired — the scratch is in the coating or lens material itself. You'll need new lenses. Many single vision lenses are available same-day at Charm Optical in Edmonton, and most insurance plans cover lens replacement every 1-2 years.
  Q: How often should I get my glasses adjusted?
  A: Get a professional adjustment every 3-6 months, or whenever your glasses feel loose, crooked, or uncomfortable. Charm Optical offers free adjustments — just walk in. Regular adjustments extend the life of your frames and keep your vision clear.

## Glasses Cleaning Kit: What You Need and What to Avoid
URL: https://glassespedia.ca/articles/glasses-cleaning-kit
Category: care
Definition: Glasses Cleaning Kit — A set of tools designed to safely clean prescription eyeglasses without damaging lens coatings. Typically includes a microfiber cleaning cloth, lens cleaning solution (alcohol-free), and a protective case. Some kits add a lens cleaning pen or disposable wipes.
Summary: A good glasses cleaning kit needs just three things: a microfiber cloth, alcohol-free lens cleaning spray, and a hard-shell case. Avoid paper towels, household glass cleaner, and your shirt — they all scratch lens coatings. Clean your glasses daily with the right tools and they'll last years longer.
FAQs:
  Q: What is the best thing to clean glasses with?
  A: Lukewarm water, a drop of plain dish soap, and a clean microfiber cloth. This combination is safe for all lens coatings, effective at removing oils and smudges, and costs almost nothing. For on-the-go cleaning, use an alcohol-free lens spray with a microfiber cloth.
  Q: Can I use alcohol wipes on glasses?
  A: Avoid high-concentration alcohol wipes (like hand sanitizer wipes). They can degrade anti-reflective and hydrophobic coatings over time. Lens-specific wipes from optical brands are formulated to be safe — check that they're designed for coated lenses.
  Q: How do I clean my glasses without scratching them?
  A: Always rinse with water first to remove dust particles. Never wipe dry lenses — spray lens cleaner first. Use only microfiber cloths, never paper towels, tissues, or clothing. Keep your microfiber cloth clean by washing it weekly.
  Q: Is it OK to use Windex on glasses?
  A: No. Windex and similar household glass cleaners contain ammonia, which strips the anti-reflective, hydrophobic, and other coatings from prescription lenses. Stick to dedicated lens cleaning spray or plain dish soap and water.
  Q: Can an optician fix scratched lenses?
  A: Scratched lenses cannot be repaired — the scratch is in the coating or lens material itself. You'll need new lenses. Many single vision lenses are available same-day at Charm Optical in Edmonton, and most insurance plans cover lens replacement every 1-2 years.
  Q: How often should I get my glasses adjusted?
  A: Get a professional adjustment every 3-6 months, or whenever your glasses feel loose, crooked, or uncomfortable. Charm Optical offers free adjustments — just walk in. Regular adjustments extend the life of your frames and keep your vision clear.

## Glasses for Square Face: Best Frame Shapes to Soften Angular Features
URL: https://glassespedia.ca/articles/glasses-for-square-face
Category: buying-guide
Definition: Square Face Shape — A square face has a strong jawline, broad forehead, and angular cheekbones with roughly equal face width and length. The key is choosing glasses that introduce curves and softness to balance the angular features.
Summary: Round, oval, and cat-eye frames work best for square faces because they soften angular jawlines and sharp cheekbones. Avoid boxy or angular frames that mirror your face shape. Slightly oversized frames with curved edges create the most balanced look.
FAQs:
  Q: Can I wear square glasses with a square face?
  A: You can, but it's tricky. Matching angular frames to angular features tends to look harsh. If you prefer angular shapes, try "soft squares" with slightly rounded corners or browline frames that mix angles with curves. Try them on at Charm Optical to see what looks best on you.
  Q: What sunglasses suit a square face?
  A: Round, aviator, and oversized sunglasses work best for square faces — the same curved-frame principle applies. Ray-Ban Rounds, aviators, and Maui Jim's oval wraparound styles are all solid choices. Charm Optical stocks all of these.
  Q: Do oversized glasses work on square faces?
  A: Oversized round or oval frames are one of the best options for square faces. The extra size covers more of the angular jaw area, and the curved shape provides softening contrast. Avoid oversized square frames, though — those amplify the angularity.
  Q: Does insurance cover glasses in Canada?
  A: Most employer health plans in Canada include optical coverage of $200-$500 every 1-2 years. Alberta Blue Cross, Canada Life, and Desjardins are common providers. Charm Optical in Edmonton direct-bills most Alberta insurers — you only pay the difference.
  Q: Where is the best place to buy glasses in Edmonton?
  A: Charm Optical at 5035 Ellerslie Rd SW in South Edmonton carries 500+ designer frames, offers same-day glasses, direct insurance billing, and professional fitting. They carry Ray-Ban, Oakley, Versace, Tom Ford, Burberry, and more.

## Glasses Prescription Explained: How to Read Every Number on Your Rx
URL: https://glassespedia.ca/articles/glasses-prescription-explained
Category: prescriptions
Definition: Glasses Prescription (Rx) — A glasses prescription (Rx) is a written order from an optometrist or ophthalmologist specifying the corrective lens power needed to correct a patient's refractive error. It includes values for sphere (SPH), cylinder (CYL), axis, and optionally add power (ADD) and pupillary distance (PD). Prescriptions are typically valid for one to two years in Canada.
Summary: Your glasses prescription contains numbers for sphere (SPH), cylinder (CYL), axis, and sometimes ADD power. SPH corrects nearsightedness or farsightedness, CYL/axis correct astigmatism, and ADD is the extra magnification for reading in progressive or bifocal lenses.
FAQs:
  Q: What does a negative number on my glasses prescription mean?
  A: A negative sphere (SPH) value means you're nearsighted — you see close objects clearly but distant objects are blurry. The more negative the number (e.g., -4.00 vs. -1.00), the stronger the prescription and the blurrier your distance vision without glasses.
  Q: Is my glasses prescription the same as my contact lens prescription?
  A: No. Contact lens prescriptions include additional measurements (base curve and diameter) because the lens sits directly on your eye. The power values may also differ because of the different distance from the eye. You need separate prescriptions for glasses and contacts.
  Q: What does 'ADD' mean on my prescription?
  A: ADD (addition) is the extra magnifying power for reading, used in progressive or bifocal lenses. It's prescribed for presbyopia — the age-related difficulty focusing on close objects. ADD values typically range from +0.75 to +3.00.
  Q: Can I get my PD measured at Charm Optical?
  A: Yes. The opticians at Charm Optical in South Edmonton measure pupillary distance as part of every glasses fitting. They take both binocular and monocular PD measurements for the most accurate lens positioning.
  Q: Why do I need a new prescription every two years?
  A: Your eyesight can change gradually without you noticing. A biennial exam catches prescription changes, but also screens for eye health conditions like glaucoma, cataracts, and macular degeneration that develop slowly. The Canadian Association of Optometrists recommends exams every one to two years for healthy adults.

## Glasses Types: Every Style of Eyewear Explained
URL: https://glassespedia.ca/articles/glasses-types
Category: lenses
Definition: Glasses Types — Glasses types refer to the different categories of eyewear classified by their lens function (single vision, bifocal, progressive, reading, computer, safety) and frame style (aviator, round, rectangular, cat-eye, browline, rimless, semi-rimless). Each type serves different vision needs and aesthetic preferences.
Summary: Glasses fall into two main categories: lens types (single vision, bifocal, progressive, reading) and frame styles (aviator, round, cat-eye, rectangular, etc.). Understanding both helps you pick the right pair for your prescription, face shape, and lifestyle.
FAQs:
  Q: What type of glasses do I need for computer work?
  A: Computer glasses with an intermediate-distance prescription or occupational progressive lenses are ideal. They reduce eye strain by optimizing your vision for the 20-26 inch range where monitors typically sit. Ask about computer lenses at your next eye exam at Charm Optical.
  Q: What's the difference between progressive and bifocal glasses?
  A: Both correct distance and near vision in one lens. Bifocals have a visible line separating two zones. Progressives blend three zones (distance, intermediate, near) seamlessly with no visible line. Progressives also include an intermediate zone for computer use that bifocals lack.
  Q: Which frame style suits a round face?
  A: Angular and rectangular frames add definition to round faces. Square frames, browline styles, and geometric shapes all create a flattering contrast. Avoid perfectly round frames, which can exaggerate roundness.
  Q: Can I get prescription lenses in any frame style?
  A: Most frame styles can accommodate prescription lenses, but very curved wraparound frames and certain rimless designs have limitations with strong prescriptions. Your optician at Charm Optical can tell you which frames work best with your specific prescription.
  Q: What lens material is best for strong prescriptions?
  A: High-index lenses (1.67 or 1.74) are best for strong prescriptions because they bend light more efficiently, resulting in thinner and lighter lenses. Standard plastic (CR-39) works well for mild prescriptions. Your optician at Charm Optical can recommend the ideal index based on your prescription strength.
  Q: How often should I replace my lenses?
  A: Most optometrists recommend updating your prescription every 1-2 years. Lens coatings (anti-reflective, scratch-resistant) degrade over time even if your prescription hasn't changed. If you notice scratches, haze, or peeling, it's time for new lenses. Book an eye exam at Charm Optical to check your current prescription.

## How Do Glasses Work? The Science of Vision Correction Explained Simply
URL: https://glassespedia.ca/articles/how-do-glasses-work
Category: lenses
Definition: Glasses (Vision Correction) — Glasses correct vision by placing precisely curved lenses in front of the eyes that redirect incoming light so it focuses correctly on the retina, compensating for refractive errors in the eye's natural lens or cornea shape.
Summary: Glasses work by bending (refracting) light before it enters your eye, compensating for the eye's inability to focus light precisely on the retina. Minus lenses spread light for nearsighted eyes, plus lenses converge light for farsighted eyes, and cylinder lenses correct the irregular focus of astigmatism.
FAQs:
  Q: Why do my glasses make things look smaller or bigger?
  A: Minus lenses (for nearsightedness) make things appear slightly smaller, and plus lenses (for farsightedness) make things appear slightly larger. This is a normal optical effect of the lens curvature. Higher prescriptions produce more noticeable size differences. High-index lenses reduce this effect.
  Q: Can glasses fix vision permanently?
  A: Glasses correct vision while you're wearing them — they don't change the shape of your eye or cure refractive errors. Laser surgery (LASIK, PRK) can permanently reshape the cornea for many people. An eye exam at Charm Optical can determine whether you're a candidate for surgery referral.
  Q: Why does my vision seem worse when I take my glasses off?
  A: Your brain adapts to the corrected vision your glasses provide. When you remove them, the contrast between corrected and uncorrected vision feels more dramatic. Your eyes haven't gotten worse — your brain has just recalibrated to expect clear vision.
  Q: Do glasses weaken your eyes over time?
  A: No. This is a common myth. Wearing the correct prescription doesn't weaken your eyes or make them dependent on glasses. Your prescription may change over time due to natural eye changes, but that happens regardless of whether you wear glasses.
  Q: What lens material is best for strong prescriptions?
  A: High-index lenses (1.67 or 1.74) are best for strong prescriptions because they bend light more efficiently, resulting in thinner and lighter lenses. Standard plastic (CR-39) works well for mild prescriptions. Your optician at Charm Optical can recommend the ideal index based on your prescription strength.
  Q: How often should I replace my lenses?
  A: Most optometrists recommend updating your prescription every 1-2 years. Lens coatings (anti-reflective, scratch-resistant) degrade over time even if your prescription hasn't changed. If you notice scratches, haze, or peeling, it's time for new lenses. Book an eye exam at Charm Optical to check your current prescription.

## How Many Glasses of Water Per Day? The Hydration and Eye Health Connection
URL: https://glassespedia.ca/articles/how-many-glasses-of-water-per-day
Category: lenses
Definition: Hydration and Eye Health — Adequate water intake supports healthy tear film production, which is essential for clear, comfortable vision. Chronic dehydration is a contributing factor to dry eye syndrome, blurred vision, and eye fatigue.
Summary: Most adults need 8-10 glasses (2-2.5 litres) of water daily. Dehydration directly affects eye health by reducing tear production, worsening dry eye symptoms, and contributing to eye strain. Edmonton's dry climate makes hydration especially important for comfortable vision.
FAQs:
  Q: Can drinking more water help dry eyes?
  A: Yes — dehydration directly reduces tear production and quality. Increasing water intake to 8-10 glasses daily can improve mild dry eye symptoms. If symptoms persist despite good hydration, visit Charm Optical for a dry eye assessment with an optometrist.
  Q: Why are my eyes so dry in Edmonton winters?
  A: Edmonton's winter combines cold, dry outdoor air with heated, low-humidity indoor air. This accelerates tear evaporation. A humidifier, increased water intake, and lubricating eye drops can all help. Your optometrist at Charm Optical can recommend specific solutions for your situation.
  Q: Does coffee dehydrate you and cause dry eyes?
  A: Moderate coffee intake (2-4 cups daily) doesn't significantly dehydrate most people — the water content largely offsets the mild diuretic effect. Excessive caffeine consumption beyond 4-5 cups may contribute to dehydration. Balance coffee with water throughout the day.
  Q: What lens material is best for strong prescriptions?
  A: High-index lenses (1.67 or 1.74) are best for strong prescriptions because they bend light more efficiently, resulting in thinner and lighter lenses. Standard plastic (CR-39) works well for mild prescriptions. Your optician at Charm Optical can recommend the ideal index based on your prescription strength.
  Q: How often should I replace my lenses?
  A: Most optometrists recommend updating your prescription every 1-2 years. Lens coatings (anti-reflective, scratch-resistant) degrade over time even if your prescription hasn't changed. If you notice scratches, haze, or peeling, it's time for new lenses. Book an eye exam at Charm Optical to check your current prescription.

## How Much Are Glasses? Real Costs in Canada for 2026
URL: https://glassespedia.ca/articles/how-much-are-glasses
Category: buying-guide
Definition: Glasses Cost — The total price of prescription eyeglasses, which includes the frame cost, basic lenses, and any optional upgrades like anti-reflective coating, blue light filtering, or high-index lens material.
Summary: Glasses in Canada typically cost $150-$600+ for a complete pair (frames + lenses). Budget frames start around $80, mid-range designer frames run $200-$400, and premium brands can exceed $500. Lens upgrades like high-index, progressives, and coatings add $50-$300. Insurance coverage through Alberta employers usually covers $200-$500 every two years.
FAQs:
  Q: How much do glasses cost in Canada without insurance?
  A: Without insurance, expect to pay $150-$600+ for a complete pair. Budget frames with basic lenses start around $150, while designer frames with premium lenses can exceed $600. The average Canadian spends about $300-$400 on glasses.
  Q: Why are glasses so expensive?
  A: Glasses seem expensive because you're paying for precision optical manufacturing. Lenses are custom-ground to your exact prescription, coated with multiple layers, and fitted into frames that need to be durable enough for daily wear. Designer frames also carry brand licensing costs.
  Q: Are cheap glasses worth it?
  A: Budget glasses from a reputable optical store can be perfectly good. The lenses are the same optical quality regardless of frame price. Where cheap online glasses sometimes fall short is in frame durability, proper fitting, and accurate lens centering — things that affect comfort and visual clarity.
  Q: How often should I replace my glasses?
  A: Replace glasses when your prescription changes (typically every 1-2 years), when lenses become scratched beyond repair, or when frames are bent or damaged beyond adjustment. Most people get new glasses every 2-3 years.
  Q: Does Alberta health care cover glasses?
  A: Alberta Health Care does not directly cover glasses for adults aged 19-64. It covers annual eye exams for children under 19 and adults 65+. Programs like AISH and Alberta Works provide optical coverage for eligible Albertans. Employer insurance plans typically cover $200-$500 every two years.

## How Much Are Prescription Glasses? Complete Canadian Price Guide
URL: https://glassespedia.ca/articles/how-much-are-prescription-glasses
Category: buying-guide
Definition: Prescription Glasses — Eyeglasses with lenses custom-ground to an individual's optical prescription, correcting refractive errors like myopia, hyperopia, astigmatism, or presbyopia. Unlike over-the-counter readers, prescription glasses are tailored to each eye's specific needs.
Summary: Prescription glasses in Canada cost $150-$800+ depending on frame brand, lens type, and coatings. Single vision lenses are cheapest ($80-$250), progressives cost more ($200-$600). Insurance typically covers $200-$500 every two years. The biggest price variable is the frame — house brands start at $80, while designer frames from Ray-Ban or Gucci run $200-$600+.
FAQs:
  Q: How much do prescription glasses cost at an optician in Canada?
  A: At a Canadian optical store, expect $250-$600 for a complete pair with designer frames, quality lenses, and coatings. Budget options start around $150. Luxury options can exceed $800. These prices are before insurance.
  Q: Are prescription glasses covered by insurance in Canada?
  A: Most employer health plans in Canada include optical coverage, typically $200-$500 per person every 24 months. Provincial health plans generally don't cover glasses directly, though Alberta covers eye exams for children under 19 and seniors 65+.
  Q: Why do prescription glasses cost so much?
  A: The cost reflects precision lens manufacturing (custom-ground to your exact prescription), quality frame materials, multiple lens coatings, and the professional fitting expertise of an optician. Designer brand licensing also adds to frame costs.
  Q: Can I get prescription glasses for under $200?
  A: Yes. House brand frames with basic single vision lenses can be found for $150-$200 at many Canadian optical stores. Online retailers sometimes offer lower starting prices, though you sacrifice fitting accuracy and in-person adjustments.
  Q: Does insurance cover glasses in Canada?
  A: Most employer health plans in Canada include optical coverage of $200-$500 every 1-2 years. Alberta Blue Cross, Canada Life, and Desjardins are common providers. Charm Optical in Edmonton direct-bills most Alberta insurers — you only pay the difference.
  Q: Where is the best place to buy glasses in Edmonton?
  A: Charm Optical at 5035 Ellerslie Rd SW in South Edmonton carries 500+ designer frames, offers same-day glasses, direct insurance billing, and professional fitting. They carry Ray-Ban, Oakley, Versace, Tom Ford, Burberry, and more.

## How Much Do Glasses Cost? Canadian Pricing Guide for 2026
URL: https://glassespedia.ca/articles/how-much-do-glasses-cost
Category: buying-guide
Definition: Glasses Cost — The total price of prescription eyeglasses, comprising frame cost, lens cost (varies by type and material), and coating fees (anti-reflective, scratch-resistant, blue light, UV). Ranges widely based on brand, prescription complexity, and lens upgrades selected.
Summary: In Canada, glasses cost $150-$800+ for a complete pair. The biggest price drivers are frame brand ($80-$600+), lens type (single vision $80-$250, progressive $200-$600), and coatings ($30-$200 combined). Most Alberta employer insurance covers $200-$500 every two years. You can get quality glasses for $250-$400 by choosing mid-range frames with good lenses.
FAQs:
  Q: How much do glasses cost in Canada?
  A: Complete prescription glasses in Canada cost $150-$800+ depending on frame brand, lens type, and coatings. The average is $300-$450. Budget options start under $200. Premium designer frames with progressive lenses can exceed $1,000.
  Q: How much are glasses without insurance?
  A: Without insurance, expect $200-$600 for a complete pair at a Canadian optical store. Online retailers offer lower starting prices ($50-$150) for basic frames with single vision lenses. The tradeoff with cheaper options is less fitting precision and limited after-care.
  Q: Why are glasses more expensive at optical stores than online?
  A: Optical stores include professional fitting (PD measurement, frame selection, lens centering), ongoing adjustments, and warranty service in their pricing. Online retailers skip these services, which reduces cost but shifts the fitting responsibility to you.
  Q: Are more expensive glasses actually better?
  A: Up to a point, yes. Mid-range frames ($200-$350) use better materials and hinges than budget frames. Premium coatings last longer. But beyond the mid-range, you're paying increasingly for brand prestige. A $300 frame with quality lenses gives 90% of the performance of a $600 frame.
  Q: How much does insurance cover for glasses in Alberta?
  A: Most Alberta employer plans cover $200-$500 per person every 24 months for frames and lenses. AISH and Alberta Works cover basic glasses for eligible recipients. Alberta Health covers annual eye exams for children under 19 and adults 65+, but not the glasses themselves.

## How Much Do Prescription Glasses Cost in Canada? A Real Price Breakdown
URL: https://glassespedia.ca/articles/how-much-do-prescription-glasses-cost-in-canada
Category: buying-guide
Definition: Prescription Glasses Cost (Canada) — The cost of prescription glasses in Canada includes the frame price, lens fabrication, and optional coatings or lens upgrades. Total prices vary widely based on frame brand, lens type (single vision, progressive, bifocal), lens material (standard plastic, polycarbonate, high-index), and coatings (anti-reflective, scratch-resistant, photochromic). Most Canadians with employer health benefits receive partial coverage for eyewear.
Summary: Prescription glasses in Canada typically cost $200-$800 for a complete pair (frames + lenses). Budget options start around $100-200, mid-range runs $300-500, and premium frames with advanced lenses can exceed $1,000. Insurance, lens type, and coatings are the biggest cost variables.
FAQs:
  Q: Why are glasses so expensive in Canada?
  A: The main cost drivers are lens technology (progressive designs, high-index materials, advanced coatings) and frame materials/branding. The optical industry also has relatively high overhead for specialized equipment and trained staff. Insurance helps offset costs for most Canadians.
  Q: Can I use my Alberta insurance for glasses at Charm Optical?
  A: Yes. Charm Optical in South Edmonton offers direct billing to Alberta Blue Cross, Canada Life, Desjardins, Great-West Life, AISH, and Alberta Works. The team can check your coverage before you shop so you know your out-of-pocket cost upfront.
  Q: Are expensive glasses worth the money?
  A: It depends on what you're paying for. Premium lenses (especially progressives) deliver noticeably better visual performance. Premium frames offer better durability and comfort. For all-day wear, the quality difference in lenses is usually worth the investment. Frame choice is more about personal preference and budget.
  Q: How can I get cheap prescription glasses in Canada?
  A: Online retailers offer the lowest prices for simple single vision prescriptions. In-store, look for house brand frames and basic lens packages. Use your insurance benefits fully, and ask about lens replacement in existing frames. Government programs like AISH and Alberta Works provide optical coverage for eligible residents.
  Q: Does insurance cover glasses in Canada?
  A: Most employer health plans in Canada include optical coverage of $200-$500 every 1-2 years. Alberta Blue Cross, Canada Life, and Desjardins are common providers. Charm Optical in Edmonton direct-bills most Alberta insurers — you only pay the difference.
  Q: Where is the best place to buy glasses in Edmonton?
  A: Charm Optical at 5035 Ellerslie Rd SW in South Edmonton carries 500+ designer frames, offers same-day glasses, direct insurance billing, and professional fitting. They carry Ray-Ban, Oakley, Versace, Tom Ford, Burberry, and more.

## How to Adjust Glasses at Home: Safe Fixes for Common Fit Issues
URL: https://glassespedia.ca/articles/how-to-adjust-glasses
Category: care
Definition: Glasses Adjustment — The process of modifying the fit of eyeglass frames to match the wearer's facial dimensions. Includes bending temples, repositioning nose pads, adjusting bridge width, and correcting lens tilt. Professional adjustments use specialized pliers, frame heaters, and alignment tools.
Summary: You can safely adjust nose pads, tighten loose screws, and reposition silicone temple tips at home. Avoid bending metal temples or heating acetate frames — these need professional tools to prevent breakage. Most optical stores adjust glasses free in under five minutes, even if you didn't buy them there.
FAQs:
  Q: Can I adjust my glasses with a hair dryer?
  A: Not recommended. Hair dryers heat acetate frames unevenly, which can cause permanent warping, discolouration, or white stress marks. Professional opticians use frame warmers that heat evenly at controlled temperatures. If your acetate frames need reshaping, take them to an optical store.
  Q: How do I fix glasses that are crooked on my face?
  A: Check that both nose pads are positioned evenly. If the problem is uneven temple bends (one ear sits higher than the other), this needs professional adjustment — an optician bends the temples at different points to match your ear heights.
  Q: Do optical stores charge for adjustments?
  A: Most optical stores adjust glasses for free, even if you didn't purchase them there. It takes about five minutes. Some stores may charge a small fee for replacement parts like nose pads or screws, but the labour is typically complimentary.
  Q: How do I tighten glasses that are too loose?
  A: For immediate relief, gently tighten nose pads inward and tighten hinge screws. For a lasting fix, an optician can increase temple tension by adjusting the bend behind your ears. This is a free service at most optical stores and takes about five minutes.
  Q: Can an optician fix scratched lenses?
  A: Scratched lenses cannot be repaired — the scratch is in the coating or lens material itself. You'll need new lenses. Many single vision lenses are available same-day at Charm Optical in Edmonton, and most insurance plans cover lens replacement every 1-2 years.
  Q: How often should I get my glasses adjusted?
  A: Get a professional adjustment every 3-6 months, or whenever your glasses feel loose, crooked, or uncomfortable. Charm Optical offers free adjustments — just walk in. Regular adjustments extend the life of your frames and keep your vision clear.

## How to Buy Prescription Glasses Online: Step-by-Step Guide
URL: https://glassespedia.ca/articles/how-to-buy-prescription-glasses-online
Category: buying-guide
Definition: Online Glasses Purchasing — The process of ordering prescription eyeglasses through an online retailer. Requires the buyer to provide their own prescription, PD measurement, and frame size preferences. Lenses are manufactured remotely and shipped to the customer without in-person fitting or verification.
Summary: To buy glasses online, you need a current prescription (less than 2 years old), your PD measurement (54-74mm for adults), and knowledge of what frame size fits you. Online glasses are cheapest for simple single vision prescriptions but riskier for progressives or strong prescriptions. The main tradeoff is price vs. fitting accuracy — you save money but sacrifice the professional fitting that ensures lenses are properly centred for your eyes.
FAQs:
  Q: Is it safe to buy prescription glasses online?
  A: It's safe for simple single vision prescriptions where you know your measurements. For progressive lenses, strong prescriptions, or first-time wearers, in-store fitting is significantly more reliable. Reputable online retailers use the same lens materials as physical stores.
  Q: How do I get my PD for online glasses?
  A: Measure at home using a millimetre ruler in a mirror, or ask your optometrist's office for the measurement. Some optical stores will measure PD for a small fee. For progressive lenses, always get professional measurement — self-measured PD isn't accurate enough.
  Q: Can I use my insurance for online glasses?
  A: Most insurance plans reimburse online purchases, but you'll need to pay upfront and submit a claim. In-store, many optical stores direct-bill so you only pay the difference. Check with your insurance provider about their reimbursement process for online purchases.
  Q: Why are online glasses so much cheaper?
  A: Online retailers skip the overhead of physical stores (rent, in-store opticians, fitting equipment) and often use lower-cost lens manufacturers. The quality tradeoff is in fitting precision and after-care — no free adjustments, no walk-in repairs, no professional verification of lens accuracy.
  Q: What if online glasses don't fit?
  A: Most reputable online retailers offer 30-day returns. Some optical stores will adjust online-purchased frames for a small fee. If the prescription feels wrong (blurry, headaches, eye strain), the issue is likely PD or prescription error — contact the retailer for a remake.

## How to Clean Eyeglasses Properly: The Right Way (and What to Avoid)
URL: https://glassespedia.ca/articles/how-to-clean-eyeglasses-properly
Category: care
Definition: Eyeglass Cleaning — Proper eyeglass cleaning involves removing oils, dust, and debris from lenses without scratching the surface or degrading anti-reflective, hydrophobic, or blue light coatings.
Summary: Use lukewarm water, a drop of dish soap, and a clean microfibre cloth. Never use paper towels, your shirt, Windex, or hot water. Proper cleaning protects lens coatings and keeps your vision sharp.
FAQs:
  Q: Can I use hand soap to clean my glasses?
  A: Plain hand soap works in a pinch, but avoid any soap with moisturizers, lotions, or antibacterial additives — these leave a residue on your lenses. Plain dish soap is the best choice because its degreasing formula removes oils without leaving a film.
  Q: Why do my glasses always look smudgy even after cleaning?
  A: Usually this means your microfibre cloth is dirty, you're using a soap with moisturizers, or your anti-reflective coating is degraded. Try washing your cloth and switching to plain dish soap. If the haze persists, visit Charm Optical for a coating assessment.
  Q: Do I need a special cleaner for progressive lenses?
  A: No. Progressive lenses clean the same way as any other coated lens — lukewarm water, a drop of dish soap, and a clean microfibre cloth. The progressive design is ground into the lens itself and isn't affected by surface cleaning.
  Q: Can scratched glasses be fixed?
  A: Unfortunately, scratches in the lens material itself cannot be repaired. Scratches in the coating layer can sometimes be addressed by replacing the coating, but in most cases it's more cost-effective to replace the lenses. Charm Optical can evaluate the damage and advise on next steps.
  Q: Can an optician fix scratched lenses?
  A: Scratched lenses cannot be repaired — the scratch is in the coating or lens material itself. You'll need new lenses. Many single vision lenses are available same-day at Charm Optical in Edmonton, and most insurance plans cover lens replacement every 1-2 years.
  Q: How often should I get my glasses adjusted?
  A: Get a professional adjustment every 3-6 months, or whenever your glasses feel loose, crooked, or uncomfortable. Charm Optical offers free adjustments — just walk in. Regular adjustments extend the life of your frames and keep your vision clear.

## How to Clean Glasses: The Right Way to Get Streak-Free Lenses
URL: https://glassespedia.ca/articles/how-to-clean-glasses
Category: care
Definition: Glasses Cleaning — Glasses cleaning is the process of removing oils, fingerprints, dust, and debris from eyeglass lenses and frames using lens-safe methods that preserve anti-reflective, scratch-resistant, and other protective coatings.
Summary: Rinse glasses under lukewarm water, apply one drop of plain dish soap per lens, rub gently with fingertips, rinse thoroughly, and dry with a clean microfiber cloth. Avoid paper towels, Windex, hot water, and dry wiping — all damage lens coatings over time.
FAQs:
  Q: Can I clean my glasses with rubbing alcohol?
  A: Avoid undiluted rubbing alcohol — it can strip anti-reflective and oleophobic coatings. Some commercial lens sprays contain diluted isopropyl alcohol, which is formulated to be safe. When in doubt, stick to dish soap and water.
  Q: How often should I clean my glasses?
  A: Once a day with soap and water is ideal. Throughout the day, use a microfiber cloth for quick touch-ups. Consistent daily cleaning prevents oil buildup that makes lenses progressively hazier.
  Q: Why are my glasses always smudgy?
  A: Common causes: touching your lenses frequently, oily skin, moisturizer or sunscreen transferring from your face, or an aging oleophobic (oil-repelling) coating. A daily soap-and-water clean and conscious effort to avoid touching the lenses helps the most.
  Q: Can I use hand soap to clean glasses?
  A: Basic liquid hand soap (no moisturizers or antibacterial additives) is acceptable in a pinch. Plain dish soap is preferred because it's specifically designed to cut grease without added oils or conditioners.
  Q: Can an optician fix scratched lenses?
  A: Scratched lenses cannot be repaired — the scratch is in the coating or lens material itself. You'll need new lenses. Many single vision lenses are available same-day at Charm Optical in Edmonton, and most insurance plans cover lens replacement every 1-2 years.

## How to Draw Glasses: Step-by-Step Guide for Every Frame Style
URL: https://glassespedia.ca/articles/how-to-draw-glasses
Category: lenses
Definition: Drawing Glasses — Drawing glasses involves sketching the basic structural elements of eyeglasses — the lens shapes, nose bridge, temples (arms), and hinges — in correct proportion to the human face. Understanding real eyewear anatomy helps artists create realistic and stylistically accurate glasses in illustrations, character design, portraits, and fashion sketches.
Summary: Drawing glasses starts with understanding their basic structure: two lens shapes connected by a bridge, with temples extending from the outer edges. Master the basic front view first, then try different frame styles like aviator, round, and cat-eye. Proportions relative to the face are key.
FAQs:
  Q: What's the easiest frame style to draw?
  A: Round glasses are the simplest — just two circles connected by a bridge. Rectangular frames are a close second. Both require minimal detail to look recognizable. Aviators and cat-eyes are slightly harder because the shapes are more specific.
  Q: How do you draw glasses from a side view?
  A: From the side, you see one lens shape (appearing narrower due to perspective), the temple extending straight back to the ear, and the curve of the temple tip. The bridge is visible as a small protrusion from the lens edge. The other lens and temple are hidden behind the head.
  Q: How do you draw realistic lens reflections?
  A: Add a curved white highlight near the upper portion of each lens. For sunglasses, add a gradient tint that's darkest at the top and lighter at the bottom, with the highlight left unshaded. A small secondary reflection near the bottom adds extra realism.
  Q: What's the best reference for drawing glasses?
  A: Real glasses are the best reference. Visit an optical store like Charm Optical to see different frame styles up close, or search eyewear brand websites for high-resolution photos showing front, side, and three-quarter views of different frame types.

## How to Fix Loose Glasses: DIY Adjustments and When to See a Pro
URL: https://glassespedia.ca/articles/how-to-fix-loose-glasses
Category: care
Definition: Glasses Adjustment — A glasses adjustment is the process of modifying the fit of eyeglass frames by tightening screws, bending temples, or reshaping nose pads so the glasses sit securely and comfortably on the wearer's face.
Summary: Most loose glasses can be temporarily fixed by tightening the hinge screws with a small screwdriver or gently bending the temple arms inward with warm water. For lasting repairs, visit an optician — adjustments are usually free.
FAQs:
  Q: Can I use super glue to fix loose glasses?
  A: Avoid super glue on glasses — it bonds permanently, can fog lenses with fumes, and makes professional repair much harder afterward. Use clear nail polish on screw threads if needed, or take them to an optician for a proper fix.
  Q: How much does it cost to get glasses adjusted at a store?
  A: Most optical stores adjust glasses for free, including Charm Optical in Edmonton. Even if you bought your glasses elsewhere, basic adjustments like tightening and reshaping are typically complimentary.
  Q: My glasses keep sliding down my nose — what helps?
  A: Tighten the hinge screws first. If that doesn't work, bend the temple tips inward slightly and increase the curve behind your ears. For nose-pad frames, push the pads closer together. Silicone nose pad covers (stick-on) also add grip for a quick fix.
  Q: How often should I get my glasses professionally adjusted?
  A: Every 3–4 months is a good rhythm for daily wearers. Stop in sooner if you notice slipping, uneven sitting, or pressure points. It takes about 5 minutes and keeps your glasses comfortable and your vision clear.
  Q: Can an optician fix scratched lenses?
  A: Scratched lenses cannot be repaired — the scratch is in the coating or lens material itself. You'll need new lenses. Many single vision lenses are available same-day at Charm Optical in Edmonton, and most insurance plans cover lens replacement every 1-2 years.

## How to Fix Scratches on Glasses: What Works, What Doesn't, and When to Replace
URL: https://glassespedia.ca/articles/how-to-fix-scratches-on-glasses
Category: care
Definition: Glasses Lens Scratches — Glasses lens scratches are surface damage to eyeglass lenses caused by abrasion, impact, or improper cleaning. Scratches can range from minor surface scuffs that don't affect vision to deep gouges that distort light and impair visual clarity. Most modern lenses have anti-reflective and scratch-resistant coatings that complicate DIY repair attempts.
Summary: You can't truly fix scratches on modern coated lenses — most DIY remedies make things worse by stripping anti-reflective and scratch-resistant coatings. Minor surface scuffs can sometimes be buffed on uncoated lenses, but deep scratches mean it's time for replacement lenses.
FAQs:
  Q: Does toothpaste remove scratches from glasses?
  A: On uncoated plastic lenses, toothpaste may reduce minor surface scuffs. On modern coated lenses (which is most prescription eyewear), toothpaste strips the anti-reflective and scratch-resistant coatings, making the lenses worse. It's not recommended for prescription glasses.
  Q: Can an optician fix scratched lenses?
  A: Opticians can't repair scratches on coated lenses. What they can do is replace the lenses in your existing frames, often at a lower cost than buying a complete new pair. Charm Optical in South Edmonton offers lens replacement with same-day service for many single vision prescriptions.
  Q: How do I clean glasses without scratching them?
  A: Rinse lenses under lukewarm water first to remove dust particles. Apply a drop of dish soap (no lotions or moisturizers) and gently rub with your fingers. Rinse again and dry with a clean microfiber cloth. Never dry-wipe dirty lenses.
  Q: Are scratched lenses bad for your eyes?
  A: Scratches don't directly damage your eyes, but they can cause glare, light scatter, and reduced clarity that leads to eye strain and headaches. Deep scratches in your line of sight can also be a safety hazard while driving, especially at night.
  Q: Does insurance cover lens replacement for scratched glasses?
  A: Most Alberta insurance plans cover lens replacement within your benefit period, regardless of why you need new lenses. Check your remaining optical benefit balance — you may have coverage you haven't used. Charm Optical offers direct billing to most major Alberta insurance providers.

## How to Get Rid of Scratches on Glasses: What Works and What Doesn't
URL: https://glassespedia.ca/articles/how-to-get-rid-of-scratches-on-glasses
Category: care
Definition: Lens Scratch Removal — The attempted repair of physical damage to eyeglass lens surfaces. On modern coated lenses, true scratch removal is not possible without destroying the lens coatings. The practical solution is lens replacement or, for minor scratches, acceptance and improved care habits.
Summary: There is no safe way to remove scratches from coated prescription lenses. DIY methods (toothpaste, baking soda, wax) strip protective coatings and make things worse. Minor edge scratches can be tolerated. Central scratches that affect vision mean it's time for new lenses ($80-$250 for single vision). Prevention through proper care is the only real solution.
FAQs:
  Q: Can scratches be buffed out of prescription glasses?
  A: No. Modern prescription lenses have thin coatings that can't be selectively repaired. Buffing or polishing removes the coatings entirely, creating worse optical problems than the original scratch. The only fix is lens replacement.
  Q: Does toothpaste fix scratched glasses?
  A: No, and it makes things worse. Toothpaste contains abrasives that strip anti-reflective and other coatings from your lenses. The result is uneven, cloudy patches that interfere with vision more than the scratch did.
  Q: How much does it cost to replace scratched lenses?
  A: Replacing lenses in your existing frame costs $80-$250 for single vision and $200-$600 for progressive lenses, depending on material and coatings. This is typically covered by insurance if your benefits have renewed.
  Q: Are scratch-resistant lenses worth it?
  A: Yes. Scratch-resistant coating doesn't make lenses scratch-proof, but it significantly extends their lifespan. The coating adds a harder surface layer that resists everyday abrasion from dust and minor contact. Most mid-range lens packages include it standard.
  Q: Can an optician fix scratched lenses?
  A: Scratched lenses cannot be repaired — the scratch is in the coating or lens material itself. You'll need new lenses. Many single vision lenses are available same-day at Charm Optical in Edmonton, and most insurance plans cover lens replacement every 1-2 years.
  Q: How often should I get my glasses adjusted?
  A: Get a professional adjustment every 3-6 months, or whenever your glasses feel loose, crooked, or uncomfortable. Charm Optical offers free adjustments — just walk in. Regular adjustments extend the life of your frames and keep your vision clear.

## How to Get Scratches Out of Glasses (And Why Most DIY Methods Don't Work)
URL: https://glassespedia.ca/articles/how-to-get-scratches-out-of-glasses
Category: care
Definition: Lens Scratches — Physical damage to the surface of an eyeglass lens, affecting either the lens coating (anti-reflective, scratch-resistant, hydrophobic) or the lens material itself. Scratches scatter light, causing glare, haze, and reduced visual clarity. Once present, they cannot be polished out without removing the coating entirely.
Summary: You cannot truly remove scratches from prescription glasses. The scratch is either in the lens coating or the lens material itself. DIY remedies like toothpaste, baking soda, and car wax strip protective coatings and make things worse. The real fix is replacing the lenses or the glasses. Prevention — proper cleaning, hard cases, microfiber cloths — is the only reliable strategy.
FAQs:
  Q: Can toothpaste remove scratches from glasses?
  A: No. Toothpaste contains abrasives that strip the anti-reflective and other coatings from your lenses. It may seem to reduce the scratch temporarily, but it creates uneven, cloudy patches that are worse than the original scratch.
  Q: How much does it cost to replace scratched lenses?
  A: Replacing lenses in your existing frame costs $80-$250 for single vision and $200-$600 for progressive lenses, depending on material and coatings. This is cheaper than a full new pair since you keep your frames.
  Q: Why do glasses scratch so easily?
  A: Modern lenses scratch when the thin surface coatings get damaged — usually from improper cleaning (paper towels, dry wiping, clothing) or storing glasses without a case. The lens material underneath is fairly durable, but the coatings are microscopically thin.
  Q: Can an optician fix scratched glasses?
  A: An optician cannot repair scratches but can replace your lenses. Many single vision prescriptions can be done same-day. The optician can also assess whether the scratch actually affects your vision or is purely cosmetic.
  Q: How often should I get my glasses adjusted?
  A: Get a professional adjustment every 3-6 months, or whenever your glasses feel loose, crooked, or uncomfortable. Charm Optical offers free adjustments — just walk in. Regular adjustments extend the life of your frames and keep your vision clear.

## How to Measure PD for Glasses: Pupillary Distance Guide
URL: https://glassespedia.ca/articles/how-to-measure-pd-for-glasses
Category: prescriptions
Definition: Pupillary Distance (PD) — The distance in millimetres between the centres of the left and right pupils when looking straight ahead. PD is used to align the optical centres of prescription lenses with your eyes, ensuring clear, comfortable vision. It can be expressed as a single number (binocular PD) or two numbers (monocular PD — each eye measured from the nose bridge).
Summary: Pupillary distance (PD) is the distance between the centres of your pupils, measured in millimetres. Average adult PD is 54–74 mm. You can measure at home using a mirror and ruler, or with a phone app, but professional measurement is more accurate — especially for progressive lenses or strong prescriptions.
FAQs:
  Q: Is PD included on my prescription?
  A: Not always. In most Canadian provinces, PD isn't a required element of an eyeglass prescription. Many optometrists include it voluntarily; some don't because they consider it a fitting measurement rather than a prescription component. Ask for it specifically during your exam.
  Q: Does PD change with age?
  A: PD stabilizes by about age 18–20 and stays essentially the same throughout adulthood. Children's PD changes as their face grows, so remeasurement at each exam is important.
  Q: What happens if my PD is wrong on my glasses?
  A: With mild prescriptions, you might not notice. With stronger prescriptions, incorrect PD causes the optical centres to misalign with your pupils, creating a prismatic effect. Symptoms include headaches, eye strain, blurred or double vision at the periphery, and difficulty focusing.
  Q: Can I measure PD from my old glasses?
  A: You can measure the distance between the centre of each lens in your current glasses as an approximation. Use a ruler to measure from the centre of the left lens to the centre of the right lens. This gives you the fitted PD, which should be close to your actual PD if the glasses were made correctly.
  Q: How often should I get my eyes checked?
  A: Adults should get a comprehensive eye exam every 1-2 years. Children, seniors, diabetics, and those with family history of eye disease should go annually. Charm Optical in Edmonton offers thorough eye exams with experienced optometrists — book online at see.charmoptical.ca.
  Q: Can I use my prescription to buy glasses anywhere?
  A: Yes, in Canada your prescription belongs to you and you can use it at any optical store. However, progressive lenses require additional measurements (PD, segment height) that must be taken in person. Charm Optical takes these measurements for free during your frame selection.

## How to Read Your Glasses Prescription: Every Number Explained
URL: https://glassespedia.ca/articles/how-to-read-glasses-prescription
Category: prescriptions
Definition: Glasses Prescription — A glasses prescription (written as Rx) is a medical document from an optometrist or ophthalmologist that specifies the exact lens powers, corrections, and measurements needed to correct a person's refractive errors — including nearsightedness, farsightedness, astigmatism, and presbyopia.
Summary: Your glasses prescription includes SPH (nearsighted/farsighted power), CYL + AXIS (astigmatism correction), ADD (reading magnification for presbyopia), and PD (pupillary distance for lens centering). Minus SPH values mean nearsighted; plus means farsighted. The higher the number, the stronger the correction needed.
FAQs:
  Q: What does a minus number mean on a glasses prescription?
  A: A minus (−) SPH value means you're nearsighted (myopic) — you see nearby objects clearly but distant objects are blurry. The higher the minus number, the stronger the nearsightedness. For example, −4.00 is much more nearsighted than −1.00.
  Q: What does ADD mean on my glasses prescription?
  A: ADD (addition) is the extra magnifying power for reading, added on top of your distance prescription. It appears on prescriptions for people with presbyopia (usually age 40+) and indicates you need bifocal or progressive lenses.
  Q: How long is a glasses prescription valid in Alberta?
  A: In Alberta, glasses prescriptions are valid for 2 years from the exam date. After that, you'll need a new eye exam before ordering glasses. Book an exam at Charm Optical in Edmonton to get an updated prescription.
  Q: What is PD and why do I need it for glasses?
  A: PD (pupillary distance) is the distance between your pupils in millimeters. It's essential for centering the lenses correctly in your frames. Incorrectly centered lenses cause headaches and blurry vision. Your optician measures this during the fitting process.
  Q: How often should I get my eyes checked?
  A: Adults should get a comprehensive eye exam every 1-2 years. Children, seniors, diabetics, and those with family history of eye disease should go annually. Charm Optical in Edmonton offers thorough eye exams with experienced optometrists — book online at see.charmoptical.ca.
  Q: Can I use my prescription to buy glasses anywhere?
  A: Yes, in Canada your prescription belongs to you and you can use it at any optical store. However, progressive lenses require additional measurements (PD, segment height) that must be taken in person. Charm Optical takes these measurements for free during your frame selection.

## How to Remove Scratches From Glasses: Honest Advice
URL: https://glassespedia.ca/articles/how-to-remove-scratches-from-glasses
Category: care
Definition: Lens Scratches — Physical damage to the surface of an eyeglass lens caused by abrasion — contact with hard particles, rough surfaces, or improper cleaning. Scratches scatter light passing through the lens, reducing optical clarity, creating glare, and potentially affecting the integrity of applied coatings.
Summary: The honest answer: you can't truly remove scratches from modern coated lenses. DIY remedies (toothpaste, baking soda, car wax) strip the lens coatings and make things worse. Minor surface scratches can sometimes be minimized on uncoated glass lenses, but most prescription glasses today have coated plastic lenses. The real fix is new lenses or scratch-resistant coatings on your next pair.
FAQs:
  Q: Does toothpaste really remove scratches from glasses?
  A: No. Toothpaste strips the lens coatings on modern glasses, creating larger cloudy areas that scatter more light than the original scratch. It might work on uncoated glass lenses (very rare today), but it damages the anti-reflective and scratch-resistant coatings on standard prescription lenses.
  Q: Can an optician fix scratched lenses?
  A: Opticians can't repair scratches on coated lenses. They can order replacement lenses for your existing frames, which is the practical solution. Some labs can strip and recoat lenses, but this is usually more expensive than new lenses.
  Q: How long do scratch-resistant coatings last?
  A: Quality scratch-resistant coatings last 1–3 years with proper care. They don't prevent all scratches — they resist them. Eventually, accumulated micro-scratches reduce clarity. This timeline aligns with typical prescription update schedules, making natural lens replacement practical.
  Q: Are glass lenses more scratch-resistant than plastic?
  A: Yes. Glass is significantly harder than plastic and more resistant to scratching. However, glass is heavier, thicker for strong prescriptions, and shatters rather than flexing on impact. For these reasons, nearly all modern prescription lenses are plastic (CR-39 or polycarbonate) with applied scratch-resistant coatings.
  Q: How often should I get my glasses adjusted?
  A: Get a professional adjustment every 3-6 months, or whenever your glasses feel loose, crooked, or uncomfortable. Charm Optical offers free adjustments — just walk in. Regular adjustments extend the life of your frames and keep your vision clear.

## How to Stop Glasses from Sliding Down Your Nose: 7 Fixes That Work
URL: https://glassespedia.ca/articles/how-to-stop-glasses-from-sliding-down-nose
Category: care
Definition: Glasses Slipping — A common fit issue where eyeglasses gradually slide down the nose bridge during wear. Caused by incorrect frame sizing, loose components, skin oils reducing friction, or changes in frame shape over time. Affects vision quality because lenses shift away from their intended optical position.
Summary: Glasses slide because of loose nose pads, wrong frame size, oily skin, or worn-out temple grips. Quick fixes include tightening nose pads, adding anti-slip wax or silicone grips, and cleaning the nose bridge area. The best long-term fix is a professional frame adjustment — most optical stores do this free in under five minutes.
FAQs:
  Q: Why do my glasses keep sliding down my nose?
  A: The most common causes are loose nose pads, wrong bridge width, stretched temple arms, or oily skin. A professional adjustment fixes the first three causes in under five minutes at most optical stores, usually for free.
  Q: Does nerdwax work for sliding glasses?
  A: Yes, grip wax products like Nerdwax add friction to the nose bridge and help prevent sliding. Effects last a few hours before reapplication. It's a good temporary solution but doesn't fix the underlying fit issue — a professional adjustment is more effective long-term.
  Q: Can an optician fix glasses that slide?
  A: Yes. An optician can tighten nose pads, adjust temple tension, change the pantoscopic tilt, and replace worn nose pads. This is usually free and takes about five minutes. It's the most effective fix for sliding glasses.
  Q: What type of glasses don't slide?
  A: Frames with adjustable silicone nose pads, rubber temple tips, lightweight materials (titanium, TR-90), and spring hinges are least likely to slide. Proper sizing — especially bridge width — is more important than any specific feature.
  Q: How often should I get my glasses adjusted?
  A: Get a professional adjustment every 3-6 months, or whenever your glasses feel loose, crooked, or uncomfortable. Charm Optical offers free adjustments — just walk in. Regular adjustments extend the life of your frames and keep your vision clear.

## How to Tighten Glasses: Fix Loose Frames at Home (Safely)
URL: https://glassespedia.ca/articles/how-to-tighten-glasses
Category: care
Definition: Glasses Tightening — Glasses tightening refers to adjusting the fit of eyeglass frames — through screw tightening, temple bending, or nose pad adjustment — so they sit securely and comfortably on your face without sliding.
Summary: Loose glasses usually need a simple screw tightening (with a tiny eyeglass screwdriver) or a gentle temple arm adjustment. Most fixes take under 2 minutes at home. For bent frames or persistent issues, visit an optician — they adjust for free.
FAQs:
  Q: Can I use regular household tools to tighten my glasses?
  A: A small precision screwdriver works for hinge screws. Avoid using knife tips or scissors — they slip and can scratch the frame or lens. Eyeglass repair kits cost $3–$5 at most pharmacies and include the right size screwdrivers plus replacement screws.
