Summary: Progressive lenses are multifocal lenses that blend three prescriptions — distance, intermediate, and near — into one seamless lens with no visible line. They're the modern replacement for bifocals, ideal for people over 40 experiencing presbyopia.
FAQs:
  Q: Are progressive lenses worth the extra cost over bifocals?
  A: For most people, yes. Progressives eliminate the visible line, provide intermediate vision for computer use, and look like regular glasses. The adjustment period is the main trade-off, but most wearers adapt within two weeks and prefer progressives once they do.
  Q: Can you get progressive lenses in any frame?
  A: Technically yes, but very small or narrow frames may not leave enough room for all three vision zones. Your optician at Charm Optical can help you choose frames that give progressive lenses enough vertical height to work properly.
  Q: How long does it take to get used to progressive lenses?
  A: Most people adapt within one to two weeks of full-time wear. The key is wearing them consistently — switching back and forth with old glasses slows the process. If you're still uncomfortable after two weeks, visit your optician for a fitting check.
  Q: Do progressive lenses work for driving?
  A: Yes. The top zone of a progressive lens is set to your distance prescription, which is what you use for driving. Some people find they need a slight head tilt to check mirrors or the dashboard, but this becomes second nature quickly.
  Q: Can I get progressive sunglasses?
  A: Absolutely. Progressive lenses can be made with tinted, polarized, or photochromic (Transitions) lens options. Ask about progressive sunglasses at your next visit to Charm Optical in South Edmonton.

## What to Do with Old Glasses: 8 Smart Options Beyond the Junk Drawer
URL: https://glassespedia.ca/articles/what-to-do-with-old-glasses
Category: care
Definition: Old Glasses Disposal & Reuse — Old glasses are previously worn prescription or non-prescription eyeglasses that are no longer in active use, typically because the wearer's prescription has changed, the frames are damaged, or they've been replaced by a newer pair. These glasses can be donated, recycled, repurposed, or responsibly disposed of rather than discarded.
Summary: Old glasses can be donated to charities like the Lions Club, recycled at optical stores, repurposed as backup pairs, or used for creative DIY projects. Don't just toss them in the trash.
FAQs:
  Q: Can I donate glasses that are scratched or damaged?
  A: Yes, many donation programs accept damaged glasses. Organizations like the Lions Club sort and refurbish donated pairs. Even if the frames are bent, the lenses might still be usable for someone. If they're truly beyond repair, recycling is the better option.
  Q: Where can I donate old glasses in Edmonton?
  A: Lions Club collection boxes are found at many Edmonton locations including community centres and some optical stores. You can also check with Charm Optical on Ellerslie Road — they can direct you to the nearest donation drop-off point.
  Q: Is it safe to wear someone else's old glasses?
  A: Only if the prescription matches your own. Wearing the wrong prescription can cause headaches, nausea, and eye strain. If you find old glasses and want to use them, have an optometrist check whether the prescription is compatible with your eyes.
  Q: Can old lenses be reused in new frames?
  A: Sometimes. If the lenses are in good condition and the shape is compatible with the new frames, an optician can often recut them to fit. This works best when switching between similarly shaped frames.
  Q: How often should I replace my glasses?
  A: Most optometrists recommend updating your prescription every one to two years. The Canadian Association of Optometrists suggests annual eye exams for adults, and your optometrist will tell you if your prescription has changed enough to warrant new lenses. Book your next eye exam at Charm Optical in South Edmonton to stay on track.

## When Were Glasses Invented? A History of Eyewear from 1286 to Today
URL: https://glassespedia.ca/articles/when-were-glasses-invented
Category: lenses
Definition: Invention of Eyeglasses — Eyeglasses were first invented as wearable vision aids in northern Italy around 1286 AD. The earliest versions were riveted reading glasses (two magnifying lenses connected by a rivet that balanced on the nose) used by monks and scholars.
Summary: The first wearable eyeglasses were invented in northern Italy around 1286, likely by an unknown craftsman in Venice or Florence. Before that, reading stones (polished quartz) magnified text as early as the 9th century. Bifocals came in 1784 (Benjamin Franklin), and progressive lenses arrived in 1959.
FAQs:
  Q: Who invented glasses first?
  A: The first wearable eyeglasses were created by an unknown craftsman in northern Italy around 1286. The Venetian glassmaking guilds and Florentine monasteries are the most likely places of origin. Salvino d'Armato is often credited but historians consider this attribution unreliable.
  Q: When were bifocal lenses invented?
  A: Benjamin Franklin invented bifocal lenses in 1784 by combining half-lenses for distance and reading into a single frame. The basic bifocal design was used for nearly 200 years before progressive lenses offered a line-free alternative in 1959.
  Q: When did glasses become common?
  A: Glasses became widely available after the invention of the printing press in 1440, which created mass demand for reading correction. By the 1700s, spectacles were common across Europe. Temple arms (1727) made them practical for all-day wear.
  Q: What were the first glasses made of?
  A: The earliest glasses (1280s) used polished quartz or beryl crystal lenses set in frames made of bone, metal, or riveted leather. Glass lenses came soon after, ground by hand in Venetian workshops. Modern plastic lenses didn't appear until the 1960s.

## Where to Buy Blue Light Glasses in Canada: Best Options Compared
URL: https://glassespedia.ca/articles/where-to-buy-blue-light-glasses
Category: buying-guide
Definition: Blue Light Glasses — Eyeglasses with lenses that filter a portion of blue light (400-500nm wavelength) emitted by digital screens, LED lighting, and sunlight. Available as a coating on prescription lenses or as non-prescription glasses. Intended to reduce digital eye strain and potentially improve sleep quality.
Summary: Blue light glasses are available at optical stores (as a lens coating on prescription glasses), online retailers (non-prescription for $15-$80), and pharmacies (basic non-prescription). For the best filtering and optical quality, add blue light coating to your prescription lenses at an optical store ($20-$60 extra). Non-prescription blue light glasses are fine for mild screen discomfort but won't correct vision problems.
FAQs:
  Q: Are blue light glasses worth it?
  A: They're worth the modest $20-$60 coating cost if you're already buying prescription glasses. As standalone non-prescription glasses, they may help with screen comfort and evening sleep, but the evidence for preventing eye damage is weak. Free alternatives like the 20-20-20 rule and screen night mode address digital eye strain more directly.
  Q: Can I add blue light coating to any prescription glasses?
  A: Yes. Blue light filtering is a lens coating that can be added to virtually any prescription lens — single vision, progressive, or bifocal. Ask your optician when ordering lenses. It's typically $20-$60 extra.
  Q: Do blue light glasses help with headaches?
  A: Some people report fewer screen-related headaches with blue light glasses, though the evidence is anecdotal rather than clinical. If screens trigger your headaches, the 20-20-20 rule, proper screen brightness, and correct prescription are more reliably effective.
  Q: What's the difference between cheap and expensive blue light glasses?
  A: Cheap versions ($15-$30) often have visible yellow tinting, inconsistent filtering, and lower optical clarity. Quality blue light coatings ($40-$80 standalone, or $20-$60 added to Rx lenses) have minimal colour distortion, better filtering consistency, and clearer optics.
  Q: Does insurance cover glasses in Canada?
  A: Most employer health plans in Canada include optical coverage of $200-$500 every 1-2 years. Alberta Blue Cross, Canada Life, and Desjardins are common providers. Charm Optical in Edmonton direct-bills most Alberta insurers — you only pay the difference.
  Q: Where is the best place to buy glasses in Edmonton?
  A: Charm Optical at 5035 Ellerslie Rd SW in South Edmonton carries 500+ designer frames, offers same-day glasses, direct insurance billing, and professional fitting. They carry Ray-Ban, Oakley, Versace, Tom Ford, Burberry, and more.

## Where to Buy Glasses Online in 2026: Canadian Options Compared
URL: https://glassespedia.ca/articles/where-to-buy-glasses-online
Category: buying-guide
Definition: Online Eyeglasses Retailer — A web-based business that sells prescription eyeglasses directly to consumers. Customers upload their prescription, select frames virtually, input their pupillary distance (PD), and receive finished glasses by mail. Major Canadian options include Clearly, Bonlook, and international sites like Zenni and EyeBuyDirect.
Summary: Online glasses retailers like Clearly, Bonlook, and Zenni offer lower prices than traditional stores, but you trade professional fitting, accurate measurements, and hands-on lens selection. Online works well for mild prescriptions and backup pairs. For strong Rx, progressives, or your primary glasses, in-store fitting is worth the premium.
FAQs:
  Q: Is it safe to buy prescription glasses online?
  A: For mild prescriptions and single vision lenses, yes — reputable retailers produce accurate glasses. For strong prescriptions, astigmatism correction, or progressive lenses, the risk of measurement errors is higher online because you're skipping professional fitting. Start with a simple order to test the waters.
  Q: Why are online glasses so much cheaper?
  A: Lower overhead (no retail space, fewer staff), direct-to-consumer manufacturing, and simpler lens options. In-store pricing includes the cost of professional fitting, adjustment services, and the expertise of a licensed optician — services that have genuine value, especially for complex prescriptions.
  Q: Can I use my Canadian prescription to order from US websites?
  A: Yes. Your prescription is yours, and most international sites accept Canadian prescriptions. Watch for currency conversion, shipping fees, and customs duties that can close the price gap. Also verify the return process for international orders.
  Q: What if my online glasses don't fit right?
  A: Most online retailers accept returns within 30 days. For minor adjustments (nose pad tweaks, temple bending), a local optical store can often help — though some charge a small fee for adjusting glasses purchased elsewhere.
  Q: Does insurance cover glasses in Canada?
  A: Most employer health plans in Canada include optical coverage of $200-$500 every 1-2 years. Alberta Blue Cross, Canada Life, and Desjardins are common providers. Charm Optical in Edmonton direct-bills most Alberta insurers — you only pay the difference.
  Q: Where is the best place to buy glasses in Edmonton?
  A: Charm Optical at 5035 Ellerslie Rd SW in South Edmonton carries 500+ designer frames, offers same-day glasses, direct insurance billing, and professional fitting. They carry Ray-Ban, Oakley, Versace, Tom Ford, Burberry, and more.

## Where to Buy Glasses: Online vs. In-Store vs. Big Box — Pros and Cons
URL: https://glassespedia.ca/articles/where-to-buy-glasses
Category: buying-guide
Definition: Where to Buy Glasses — Prescription glasses can be purchased from independent optical stores, chain optical retailers (like LensCrafters, Hakim Optical), online retailers (like Clearly, Zenni, EyeBuyDirect), warehouse clubs (like Costco), and department store optical centres. Each channel offers different trade-offs in price, selection, fitting quality, and after-purchase service.
Summary: You can buy glasses online (cheapest), at local optical stores (best fitting and service), at big box chains (mid-range), or at warehouse clubs (bargain frames). The best choice depends on your prescription complexity, budget, and how much you value professional fitting.
FAQs:
  Q: Is it safe to buy glasses online?
  A: For simple single vision prescriptions, online is generally fine. For progressive lenses, complex prescriptions, or children's glasses, in-person fitting is strongly recommended. The risk with online is inaccurate measurements leading to lenses that don't perform well.
  Q: Why are glasses cheaper online than in stores?
  A: Online retailers have lower overhead — no retail space, fewer staff, and they often use their own labs with lower-cost lens options. In-store prices include the cost of professional fitting, adjustments, after-sale service, and higher-quality lens options.
  Q: Can I take my prescription to any store?
  A: Yes. In Canada, your prescription is yours by law. You can fill it at any optical store, chain, warehouse club, or online retailer. Just make sure the prescription is current — most are valid for two years.
  Q: Where's the best place to buy glasses in Edmonton?
  A: For personalized service and expert fitting, independent stores like Charm Optical on Ellerslie Road offer the best experience. For budget options, Costco Optical is competitive. For maximum variety, online retailers like Clearly offer the widest selection at the lowest prices.
  Q: Does insurance cover glasses in Canada?
  A: Most employer health plans in Canada include optical coverage of $200-$500 every 1-2 years. Alberta Blue Cross, Canada Life, and Desjardins are common providers. Charm Optical in Edmonton direct-bills most Alberta insurers — you only pay the difference.
  Q: Where is the best place to buy glasses in Edmonton?
  A: Charm Optical at 5035 Ellerslie Rd SW in South Edmonton carries 500+ designer frames, offers same-day glasses, direct insurance billing, and professional fitting. They carry Ray-Ban, Oakley, Versace, Tom Ford, Burberry, and more.

## Where to Get Prescription Glasses in Canada: All Your Options
URL: https://glassespedia.ca/articles/where-to-get-prescription-glasses
Category: buying-guide
Definition: Prescription Glasses Retailers — Businesses that sell prescription eyeglasses, including independent optical stores, chain optical retailers, online eyewear companies, big-box store optical departments, and optometrist-affiliated dispensaries. Each offers different tradeoffs in price, selection, service, and fitting quality.
Summary: You can buy prescription glasses at independent optical stores, chain retailers (LensCrafters, Hakim Optical), online (Clearly, Zenni), big-box stores (Costco, Walmart), or directly from your optometrist's office. Independent stores offer the best fitting expertise and brand selection. Online is cheapest for simple prescriptions. Big-box stores balance price and convenience. Your choice depends on prescription complexity, budget, and how much fitting expertise you need.
FAQs:
  Q: What is the cheapest place to buy prescription glasses in Canada?
  A: Online retailers like Zenni and EyeBuyDirect offer the lowest starting prices (under $50 complete). In physical stores, Costco Optical typically has the best pricing. The tradeoff with cheaper options is less personalized fitting and limited after-care.
  Q: Can I buy glasses without going to an optician?
  A: You can buy frames online and have lenses made remotely if you have a current prescription and know your PD. However, you'll miss professional fitting, which affects comfort, visual accuracy, and lens longevity. For simple prescriptions it's workable; for progressives or strong Rx it's risky.
  Q: Is it better to buy glasses at the eye doctor's office?
  A: Optometrist offices offer convenience (exam + glasses in one visit) and their opticians understand your prescription well. However, they sometimes have smaller frame selections and less competitive pricing than independent optical stores. It depends on the specific office.
  Q: Does Costco have good glasses?
  A: Costco Optical offers good quality lenses (often Essilor brand) at competitive prices, with licensed opticians on staff. Frame selection is more limited than specialized optical stores, and the higher-volume environment means less personalized fitting time. For standard prescriptions, it's excellent value.
  Q: Does insurance cover glasses in Canada?
  A: Most employer health plans in Canada include optical coverage of $200-$500 every 1-2 years. Alberta Blue Cross, Canada Life, and Desjardins are common providers. Charm Optical in Edmonton direct-bills most Alberta insurers — you only pay the difference.
  Q: Where is the best place to buy glasses in Edmonton?
  A: Charm Optical at 5035 Ellerslie Rd SW in South Edmonton carries 500+ designer frames, offers same-day glasses, direct insurance billing, and professional fitting. They carry Ray-Ban, Oakley, Versace, Tom Ford, Burberry, and more.

## Which Glasses Suit My Face? A Shape-by-Shape Buying Guide
URL: https://glassespedia.ca/articles/which-glasses-suit-my-face
Category: buying-guide
Definition: Face Shape Glasses Guide — Choosing glasses that suit your face involves matching frame shapes to your facial proportions — generally using contrast (angular frames for round faces, curved frames for angular faces) while ensuring proper fit through measurements of frame width, bridge, and temple length.
Summary: The core principle: choose frames that contrast your face shape. Round faces suit angular frames, square faces suit curved frames, oval faces suit almost everything. But fit and proportion matter just as much as shape — a well-fitted "wrong" shape often looks better than a poorly-fitted "right" one.
FAQs:
  Q: What if I can't tell what face shape I have?
  A: Many people have a combination of features that don't fit neatly into one category — that's normal. Visit Charm Optical and the team will help assess your proportions and recommend frames that complement your specific features, regardless of which "category" your face falls into.
  Q: Can I break the face shape rules?
  A: Absolutely. The guidelines exist to help narrow choices, not restrict them. If you try on a pair that "shouldn't" work for your face shape but it looks great, wear it. Personal style and confidence matter more than any rule.
  Q: Should I match my glasses to my hairstyle?
  A: Hairstyle affects the overall look but shouldn't override face shape considerations. Glasses sit on your face permanently while hairstyles change. Choose frames based on your face shape and features first, then adjust your hair around them if needed.
  Q: Does insurance cover glasses in Canada?
  A: Most employer health plans in Canada include optical coverage of $200-$500 every 1-2 years. Alberta Blue Cross, Canada Life, and Desjardins are common providers. Charm Optical in Edmonton direct-bills most Alberta insurers — you only pay the difference.
  Q: Where is the best place to buy glasses in Edmonton?
  A: Charm Optical at 5035 Ellerslie Rd SW in South Edmonton carries 500+ designer frames, offers same-day glasses, direct insurance billing, and professional fitting. They carry Ray-Ban, Oakley, Versace, Tom Ford, Burberry, and more.

## Work Glasses: The Best Eyewear for Office, Computer, and On-the-Job Use
URL: https://glassespedia.ca/articles/work-glasses
Category: lenses
Definition: Work Glasses — Work glasses are prescription or non-prescription eyeglasses specifically designed or optimized for workplace visual tasks. This includes computer glasses (tuned for screen distance), occupational progressive lenses (with wider intermediate zones), anti-fatigue lenses (for younger workers with digital strain), and prescription safety glasses (for hazardous work environments).
Summary: Work glasses are eyewear optimized for your job's visual demands. Office workers benefit from computer lenses or occupational progressives. Tradespeople need prescription safety glasses. The right pair reduces eye fatigue, neck strain, and headaches.
FAQs:
  Q: Can I use my regular glasses for computer work?
  A: You can, but dedicated computer glasses or occupational lenses are more comfortable for long screen sessions. Regular distance glasses or standard progressives aren't optimized for the intermediate distance where monitors sit, which forces your eyes to compensate and leads to fatigue.
  Q: Does my employer have to pay for work glasses?
  A: In Alberta, employers must provide safety eyewear for hazardous work environments. For office computer glasses, coverage varies by employer. Some benefit plans cover a second pair for workplace use. Check with your HR department or insurance provider.
  Q: Are computer glasses the same as blue light glasses?
  A: No. Computer glasses are prescription lenses optimized for screen distance. Blue light glasses simply filter certain light wavelengths. Computer glasses address the actual cause of screen-related eye strain (focusing effort), while blue light glasses do not. Ask about the difference at Charm Optical.
  Q: How much do work glasses cost in Canada?
  A: Computer glasses with single vision lenses typically cost $200-400 including frames. Occupational progressives run $400-700+. Prescription safety glasses are $200-500+. Many of these costs are partially covered by employer insurance plans in Alberta.
